1: The Nazirite legislation opens with the standard revelation formula — YHWH speaking to Moses — signaling its divine authority. The placement of the Nazirite law here, between the purity legislation of Numbers 5 and the Aaronic blessing of Numbers 6:22-27, is not accidental: the chapter addresses voluntary individual consecration, framed by communal purity on one side and communal priestly blessing on the other. The Nazirite law governs an uncommon but legitimate form of heightened personal holiness within the covenant community.
2:
The law is addressed to both men and women (ish o ishah): the Nazirite vow is open regardless of gender. This is unusual in a legal corpus that often distinguishes by sex; both men and women can voluntarily assume the highest purity grade available to laypersons. The root nazar (H5139) — to dedicate, set apart, consecrate — gives the Nazirite their name. The Nazirite sets themselves apart la-YHWH (for YHWH), not for a human master or temple institution. This is personal, voluntary covenantal consecration.

In the ANE, Mesopotamian temple-devotee categories (the Old Babylonian nāditum women of Nippur and Sippar, for example) involved women dedicated to temple service under restrictions comparable to those of the Nazirite — celibacy, property management for the temple, ritual purity requirements. But the Nazirite institution is open to any Israelite, male or female, free or slave, and is not permanently institutional but temporally bounded by the individual's vow.
3: The grape prohibition is threefold and total: no wine (yayin), no beer (shekhar), no vinegar made from either, no grape juice (mishrat anavim), no fresh grapes, no dried grapes. The comprehensiveness is remarkable — not just fermented products but the unfermented fruit itself is forbidden. The abstention thus goes beyond an anti-intoxicant concern: grapes in any form are excluded. In Canaan and across the ANE, the grape was the primary agricultural symbol of settled, prosperous civilization — wine was the currency of hospitality, celebration, and trade. The Nazirite's total grape abstention is a visible separation from the rhythms of ordinary Canaanite-influenced culture.
4: 'Throughout the entire period of the vow' — the comprehensive temporal scope is emphasized three times across Numbers 6:4-8. Not just specific occasions but every day of the vow is governed by these prohibitions. The prohibition extends to grape skins (zag) and seeds (chartzanim) — the least desirable parts of the grape, the residue of winemaking. If even these are forbidden, the point is not about grape by-products per se but about the principle of total separation: the Nazirite's body is wholly committed to YHWH's service, not partially adjusted for it.
5: The uncut hair is the Nazirite's most visible bodily sign. Gadel pera se'ar rosho — 'he shall let grow freely the locks of his head' — pera (H6545) suggesting loose, unbound, flowing hair. In the ANE, uncut hair could signal multiple social statuses: warriors' strength (Samson, Judges 16:17), royal dignity, or ritual dedication. For the Nazirite, the growing hair is a living symbol worn on the body: a public, daily visible declaration of consecrated status. The Nazirite cannot hide his vow; it grows visibly on his head. The phrase qadosh hu la-YHWH (he is holy to YHWH) appears in Numbers 6:5 and Numbers 6:8 — holiness is not achieved by the vow but recognized by YHWH as the vow's condition and consequence.
6: The corpse-defilement prohibition places the Nazirite at precisely the purity level of the high priest. Leviticus 21:11 forbids the high priest from going near any dead body, even that of a parent; ordinary priests are permitted to touch family members' corpses (Leviticus 21:1-4). The Nazirite — a layperson — adopts the high-priestly standard: no corpse contact whatsoever during the vow period. This is the single most dramatic marker of the Nazirite's elevated purity status: for the duration of the vow, an ordinary Israelite inhabits the sacred purity domain normally reserved for the chief priest alone.
7: The family exceptions given to ordinary priests (Leviticus 21:1-4) are explicitly withdrawn from the Nazirite: even if father, mother, brother, or sister dies, he shall not go near them. The rationale is given immediately: ki nezer elohav al-rosho — 'because the crown/consecration of his God is on his head.' The Nazirite's hair is described as a nezer (H5145, crown, consecration) — the same word used for the golden crown on the high priest's turban (Exodus 29:6; Leviticus 8:9) and for a king's crown (2 Samuel 1:10). The Nazirite's uncut hair is a living crown, marking him as one whose relationship with YHWH takes precedence over family bonds — even the most urgent family bonds of mourning.
8: 'The Nazirite is holy to YHWH throughout the entire period of his separation.' The repetition of this formula (cf. Numbers 6:5) frames the central prohibitions. Qadosh (H6944, holy, set apart) is not a moral achievement but a covenantal status: the Nazirite has been set apart to YHWH, and that status of separation governs every day of the vow. The three prohibitions — grape abstention, uncut hair, no corpse contact — are not arbitrary disciplines but structured expressions of this holiness: separation from the socially ordinary (grapes), visible bodily marking (hair), and separation from the realm of death (corpses).
9: Sudden corpse defilement is treated as an emergency requiring a restart of the entire vow period. Nefesh met (H5315+H4191, a dead soul/corpse) — if someone dies unexpectedly near the Nazirite, their consecrated head is defiled. The defilement is immediate and complete; there is no partial defilement. The protocol: shave the head on the seventh day of cleansing. The seven-day cleansing period mirrors the purification cycles elsewhere in purity law (cf. Numbers 19:11-13 for corpse impurity). The shaved head removes the visible sign of the vow that has been defiled — a necessary reset before reconsecration.
10: On the eighth day — the first day of the new counting — the Nazirite brings two turtledoves or two young pigeons to the tent of meeting. The bird pair (two doves or two pigeons) is the affordable offering designated for those who cannot bring larger animals (cf. Leviticus 5:7; Numbers 12:8 — the same pair used for purification from various impurities). The priest receives the Nazirite at the entrance of the tent of meeting: the threshold of the covenantal space is both the site of defilement's resolution and the Nazirite's reentry into consecrated status.
11: One bird serves as a sin offering (chatta't), one as a burnt offering (olah). The atonement covers the defilement incurred by the corpse contact, even if it was accidental. Lekhaper alav me'asher chata al-hanafesh — 'to make atonement for him because he sinned by contact with the soul/dead.' Corpse contact, even involuntary, is a form of cultic sin requiring expiation. The phrase 'sinned by contact with the dead' applies a moral-cultic category (sin) to what might seem like a purely physical event: in Israel's purity theology, defilement and sin overlap in ways that resist modern categorical separation. The day of restarting the consecration is the day of atonement itself.
12: The vow must be restarted from the beginning: the Nazirite re-dedicates himself l'YHWH for the full term. V'hayyamim harishonim yippelu — 'the first days shall fall/be void.' All previous days of consecration are legally null: they do not count toward the total. A year-old male lamb is required as a guilt offering (asham, H817). The zero-tolerance policy for defilement during the Nazirite period — no partial credit, full restart — communicates the absolute nature of the consecration. Holiness is not divisible: a partially kept Nazirite vow is not a partially effective vow but an invalid one.
13: 'This is the law of the Nazirite' — the completion ceremony begins. Torah haNazir introduces the culminating procedure when the vow's days are complete. V'huva el-petach ohel mo'ed — 'he shall be brought to the entrance of the tent of meeting.' The Nazirite does not come as just any worshipper; he is brought (passive), accompanied to the threshold of the covenantal space where his vow originated (cf. Numbers 6:10). The entrance of the tent of meeting frames the vow's entire arc: beginning in the community, arriving at YHWH's threshold, and being released back into normal life.
14: The completion offering is the most elaborate in the Nazirite law: one unblemished year-old male lamb for a burnt offering (olah), one unblemished year-old female lamb for a sin offering (chatta't), and one unblemished ram (ayil tamim) for a fellowship offering (shelamim). The combination of all three major sacrifice types — olah, chatta't, shelamim — marks the Nazirite's completion as a comprehensive liturgical act: the vow's conclusion touches every dimension of Israel's sacrificial theology: complete self-offering (olah), expiation (chatta't), and restored communion (shelamim).
15: The bread basket (sal matzot) accompanies the animal offerings: unleavened fine-flour loaves mixed with oil, and unleavened wafers brushed with oil. Grain offerings (minchah) and drink offerings (nesekh) complete the set. This is the same basket structure as the ordination offerings for priests (cf. Leviticus 8:2, 26; Exodus 29:2) — the Nazirite's completion ceremony structurally parallels priestly ordination, fitting for a vow that has maintained priestly-grade purity throughout its duration. The Nazirite who completes the vow is in a sense being 'deordained' back into ordinary life.
16: The priest presents all the offerings before YHWH. V'hikriv lifnei YHWH — the repeated 'before YHWH' language emphasizes the divine presence at the offering. The priest performs the sin offering and burnt offering on behalf of the Nazirite — the same priestly mediation that characterizes all major sacrificial acts. The priest's role is essential: the Nazirite cannot complete the vow unilaterally but requires priestly representation before YHWH.
17: The ram is offered as a fellowship offering (shelamim), the most communal of the sacrificial forms — portions go to YHWH (fat burned), to the priest, and to the worshipper. The shelamim marks the Nazirite's re-entry into ordinary communal life: having spent the vow period separated, the fellowship offering is the meal of reunion. V'hikriv et-sal hamatzot — the bread basket is also offered with the ram. The grain offering and drink offering complete the total sacrifice set. The Nazirite's final day at the tabernacle is a day of intensive sacrificial activity.
18: At the entrance of the tent of meeting, the Nazirite shaves his consecrated head — the culminating act of the vow. V'laqach et se'ar rosh nizro — 'he shall take the hair of his consecrated head' and place it on the fire under the fellowship offering. The hair that was the visible symbol of the vow throughout its duration now becomes the final offering: it is burned. This burning of the hair is not a purification from defilement but a consecrated offering of the vow itself. The Nazirite does not simply stop being a Nazirite; he ceremonially offers up the sign of his Nazirite status to YHWH.
19: The priest takes the boiled shoulder of the ram (zeroa' mevushal), one unleavened loaf, and one unleavened wafer from the basket and places them in the Nazirite's hands. The gesture of placing food in the Nazirite's hands echoes the priestly ordination ceremony (Exodus 29:24; Leviticus 8:27-29), where the portions were similarly placed in the priests' hands for the wave offering. The Nazirite is performing a lay version of a priestly rite at the moment of his vow's completion.
20: The priest performs a wave offering (tenufah, H8573) before YHWH, waving the shoulder and the bread. The waved portions are designated as qodesh hu lakohen — 'it is holy to the priest' — a priestly portion. Al hanazir lo yayin lishto' — 'after that, the Nazirite may drink wine.' These six words end the Nazirite's separation: the grape prohibition that structured the entire vow period is formally lifted. The Nazirite returns to ordinary Israelite life. Having been set apart and released, the individual Israelite has enacted a temporary journey into the domain of holiness and returned.
21: 'This is the law of the Nazirite who vows his offering to YHWH according to his Nazirite vow' — the closing formula mirrors the opening of Numbers 6:13 (torah haNazir). Whatever the Nazirite can additionally afford beyond the mandatory offering, he may bring. The minimum is specified; generosity is uncapped. Al pi nidro asher yiddor — 'according to the vow he vows': the Nazirite's obligation is self-imposed and self-defined in its duration, yet regulated by divine prescription in its liturgical expression. The law closes with the dual affirmation: the Nazirite law governs his offering, and YHWH's law governs his separation.
22: A second revelation formula opens the Aaronic blessing section — YHWH again speaking to Moses. The transition from Nazirite law (individual voluntary consecration) to the priestly blessing (priestly act for the whole community) is significant: both address the question of how YHWH's holiness is communicated to Israel, but through opposite vectors. The Nazirite is an individual ascending toward YHWH's holiness; the Aaronic blessing is YHWH's holiness descending onto the whole community through priestly mediation.
23: The blessing is addressed to 'Aaron and his sons' — the priestly line collectively. Ko tevarakhu et b'nei yisrael — 'thus/in this manner you shall bless the Israelites.' Ko (thus, in this way) signals that the verbal formula is prescribed precisely: the priests are not to improvise a blessing but to pronounce the words YHWH has given them. The priestly blessing is not a spontaneous wish but a transmission: the words belong to YHWH, and the priests are their authorized conduit. Amor lahem — 'say to them' — the blessing is spoken directly to the people, not about them.
24:
Yevarekh'kha YHWH v'yishm'rekha — 'May the LORD bless you and keep you.' The first and shortest of the three lines (three Hebrew words). Barak (H1288, bless) — to kneel before, to speak well of, to confer blessing: the term encompasses divine favor expressed in material and relational abundance. Shamar (H8104, keep, guard, protect) — to watch over, preserve from harm. In the ANE, divine 'keeping' language was associated with royal protection: the god or king who 'keeps' their people guards them as a shepherd guards a flock. The Aaronic blessing invokes YHWH as Israel's royal guardian-protector.

The two tiny rolled silver amulets found at Ketef Hinnom (Jerusalem, c. 600 BCE) by archaeologist Gabriel Barkay in 1979 preserve versions of Numbers 6:24-26. They are the oldest surviving manuscripts of any biblical text — predating the Dead Sea Scrolls by approximately 400 years and confirming that this blessing was in use in First Temple Jerusalem. The amulets were likely worn as protection, attached to the body as a visible expression of faith in YHWH's blessing.
25:
Ya'er YHWH panav elekha vikhuneka — 'May the LORD make his face shine upon you and be gracious to you.' The second line (five Hebrew words). Or panav — the shining face — is one of the Hebrew Bible's most powerful images for divine favor. In the ANE, the radiance of a deity's face was associated with protective presence and royal approval. The Babylonian sun-god Shamash was depicted with light rays emanating from his shoulders; the Egyptian pharaoh's face shone as a visible sign of divine appointment. Israel transforms this solar imagery: YHWH's shining face is not astral but relational — a person turning their face toward another in attention and favor. The hiding of YHWH's face (haster panim) is, correspondingly, the most severe form of divine abandonment (cf. Psalms 13:1; Numbers 27:9; Numbers 88:14).

Khanan (H2603, be gracious, show favor) — the word from which chen (grace/favor) and Hannah (gracious one) derive. Divine graciousness in the biblical tradition is the unmerited extension of favor to the undeserving — a characteristic act of YHWH's covenant loyalty that cannot be reduced to reward.
26:
Yissa YHWH panav elekha v'yasem lekha shalom — 'May the LORD lift up his face toward you and grant you peace.' The third and longest line (seven Hebrew words), climaxing in shalom. The tricolon structure of the blessing in Hebrew — three words, five words, seven words — is an ascending literary pattern, a formal feature of ancient Near Eastern benedictions that builds to its climax. Shalom (H7965) is the final and comprehensive word: peace, wholeness, completeness, well-being, the condition of flourishing in right relationship with YHWH, creation, and community. The blessing begins with protection and ends with shalom — the full range from basic security to complete flourishing.

Lifting the face (nasa panim) is the counterpart to hiding it: YHWH turns his full attention toward Israel, regarding them with open favor rather than averted displeasure. In ancient diplomatic language, a king who 'lifted his face' toward a petitioner granted their request. The Aaronic blessing invokes this idiom to describe YHWH's covenantal disposition toward Israel: perpetual royal attention, not occasional notice.
27: V'samu et shemi al b'nei yisrael — 'they shall put my name on the Israelites.' The priestly act of blessing is redescribed in its theological essence: the priests are name-givers. The shem (H8034, name) placed on Israel is YHWH's own name — not a label but a presence and identity. In the ANE, a king's name placed on a city or a person indicated ownership, protection, and identification with the king's authority. YHWH's name placed on Israel by the priests means that Israel is publicly identified as belonging to YHWH, carrying his identity in the world. Va'ani avarakhem — 'and I will bless them.' The final word: YHWH himself is the agent of blessing. The priests pronounce; YHWH acts. The formula does not merely wish Israel well — it is YHWH's own commissioned act of covenant blessing, performed through priestly speech.
